Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ng Tang Hock

Facts.Text: Ng Tang Hock married Chew Kar Lay Wendy on 21 June 1995.
Ng Tang Hock co-founded Teelek Realty Pte Ltd in 1996 with Wendy. At inception, they each held 50% of the shares and were both directors.
By August 2011, Ng Tang Hock had advanced interest-free unsecured loans totaling $12,564,000 to Teelek Realty.
In September 2011, the marital relationship between Ng Tang Hock and Wendy broke down, leading to their separation.
On 1 October 2011, the $12,564,000 loans previously recorded as owing to Ng Tang Hock were reclassified in the company ledger as loans owing to Wendy.
On 7 August 2012, Ng Tang Hock resigned as a director of Teelek Realty, and his daughter Shirlyn was appointed to the board in his place.
In July 2015, Wendy transferred one share in Teelek Realty to each of her children, Shirlyn and Eugene, without issuing a transfer notice to Ng Tang Hock.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the internal reclassification of director loans and the transfer of shares to family members, conducted without the participation or consent of a 50% shareholder, constitute a departure from fair dealing.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Section 216 of the Companies Act, a shareholder may seek relief where the affairs of the company are being conducted in a manner oppressive to a member or in disregard of their interests.
Party_Details.Application: A co-founder provided the entirety of the start-up capital for an investment company. Following a domestic breakdown, the other director reclassified his substantial loans to her own credit and diluted his voting power by transferring shares in breach of the company's Articles of Association. The company also stopped holding formal meetings or providing audited accounts, altering the governance and economic balance of the entity.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Wendy had wrongfully reclassified the plaintiff's loans as loans from herself without any genuine agreement or proper accounting entries. The judge held that her subsequent withdrawal of SGD 12,564,000 from the company on the basis of that reclassification was a misappropriation of company funds. Regarding the share transfers, the court concluded that Teelek Realty's Articles required a transfer notice and a right of first refusal for the other shareholder, and that the 2015 transfers were in clear breach of these Articles. In assessing the oppression claim, the court held that Teelek Realty's affairs had been conducted in a manner that disregarded the plaintiff's interests, including the loan reclassification, the invalid share transfers, and the failure to hold physical AGMs. The judge held that these acts constituted breaches of directors' duties. Instead of ordering a buyout, the court ordered that Teelek Realty be wound up to allow independent liquidators to investigate the accounts and affairs, and ordered Wendy to return SGD 12,564,000 to the company.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Chew Kar Lay (Wendy)

Facts.Text: Chew Kar Lay Wendy was a co-founder, 50% shareholder, and director of Teelek Realty Pte Ltd.
Wendy directed the reclassification of $12,564,000 in the company's general ledger, moving the debt from Ng Tang Hock's name to her own.
Between 2012 and 2018, Wendy withdrew $62,381,129.46 from the company's account, with $12,564,000 of that sum recorded as repayment of the reclassified loans.
Wendy executed share transfers of one share each to her children, Shirlyn and Eugene, in July 2015.
As director, Wendy approved the share transfers to her children without the knowledge of the other 50% shareholder.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches fiduciary duties by reclassifying a co-shareholder's loans to her own benefit and bypassing constitutional requirements for share transfers.
Party_Details.Rule: A director must act in good faith in the best interests of the company. Acts that favor one shareholder at the expense of another in a manner that departs from fair dealing may constitute oppression.
Party_Details.Application: A director and equal shareholder utilized her control over the company's ledgers to take credit for loans she did not provide and then used that credit to withdraw millions of dollars. She further consolidated board control by appointing her children and giving them shares in violation of the pre-emption rights held by the co-founder.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Wendy's evidence was inconsistent and that the reclassification of the loans was a fabrication unsupported by any genuine agreement. The judge determined that her withdrawal of SGD 12,564,000 based on this reclassification was a misappropriation of company funds. The court also held that her 2015 share transfers were in breach of the company's Articles because no transfer notice was provided. The judge concluded that Wendy had breached her duties as a director by failing to hold proper AGMs and by using the company's accounts as conduits for her own funds. These actions justified relief under the oppression framework.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ng Pei Ling Shirlyn

Facts.Text: Ng Pei Ling Shirlyn was appointed as a director of Teelek Realty in August 2012.
Shirlyn received one share in the company from her mother and participated in a board decision to approve that transfer.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director who permits the company to be managed in a way that disregards the rights of a major shareholder is liable for oppressive conduct.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors have a duty to exercise due care and diligence. A director who permits the company's affairs to be conducted in an oppressive manner may be found to have participated in the oppression.
Party_Details.Application: A daughter was appointed to the board following her father's resignation. She signed board resolutions that approved share transfers conducted in breach of the company's constitution, which excluded her father from his right of first refusal.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the 2015 share transfer to Shirlyn was invalid as it breached the company's Articles. Although Shirlyn was largely passive and left decisions to her mother, the court concluded that she had breached her duties as a director to act with due care and diligence. By failing to ensure compliance with the Articles and disregarding the plaintiff's interests as a shareholder, her conduct formed part of the broader oppressive environment that justified a winding-up order.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ng Jin Ping Eugene

Facts.Text: Ng Jin Ping Eugene was appointed as a director of Teelek Realty in September 2014.
Eugene received one share in the company from his mother and joined the board decision that approved the transfer.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director's failure to oversee board decisions involving share transfers contributes to a finding of oppression.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must ensure that corporate actions, such as share transfers, are carried out in accordance with the company's constitution.
Party_Details.Application: A son joined the board in 2014 and participated in board approvals for share transfers to himself and his sister that bypassed the pre-emption rights of the co-founder. This occurred while substantial company funds were being reclassified for the benefit of his mother.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the transfer of one share to Eugene was invalid for non-compliance with the Articles of Association. Similar to his sister, Eugene was found to be a largely passive director. However, the court concluded that this passivity constituted a breach of his duty to act in the company's interests and with due care. The court treated the invalid transfers and his failure to protect the plaintiff's shareholder interests as part of the oppressive conduct under section 216.
